retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Caries and Periodontal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: CBCT is not recommended as a routine method to evaluate periodontal bone support. However, CBCT is more accurate in assessing bone defects and furcation lesions than conventional two-dimensional intraoral radiographs. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Endodontics
retrieved_docs.content: A small FOV CBCT should be considered in endodontics in cases where lower-dose conventional radiography does not provide sufficient information and if the use of CBCT is likely to change the diagnosis and treatment plan. [12] The potential benefits of CBCT over conventional radiographs must justify the higher levels of radiation exposure. [12] The European Society of Endodontology recommends a small FOV CBCT in the following cases if evaluation with conventional radiographs is inconclusive or insufficient. [12] Assessment and management of dentoalveolar trauma. Evaluation of complex root canal systems before endodontic therapy. Inspection of complex root canal anatomy. Evaluation of endodontic complications like post perforation. Evaluation of root resorption. Identification of obliterated root canals. Detection of periradicular bone changes that suggest root fractures. Presurgical review before endodontic surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Caries and Periodontal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Digital radiographs may provide a better definition than conventional radiographs to evaluate alveolar bone levels. [10] Full-mouth periapical and panoramic radiographs are used to visualize the periapical tissues and the entire length of the roots and stage periodontal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Caries and Periodontal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Notably, CBCT provides information in 3 dimensions but has a lower resolution than intraoral radiographs. Furthermore, metallic restorations in the path of the X-ray beam produce dark stains creating caries-like radiolucencies on the crowns of other teeth or even masking true carious lesions. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Caries and Periodontal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Bitewings radiographs are the most appropriate method to evaluate for dental caries and should be considered even in preschool children. CBCTs should not be used as a routine method for detecting caries; however, if a CBCT is done for another reason, the presence of caries must be evaluated too. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Function -- How Does Cone Beam Computed Tomography (CBCT) Work?
retrieved_docs.content: Cone beam computed tomography uses an imaging scanner designed for imaging the head and neck and can produce 3D scans of the maxillofacial skeleton. The machine used in cone beam computed tomography is similar in size to the one used for panoramic radiography. Instead of a linear array of detectors, cone beam computed tomography machines use a 2D planar sensor. X-rays are emitted as a large cone covering the area of the head being examined.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Caries and Periodontal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of periodontal disease is based mainly on clinical examination, further supplemented by radiographic evaluation, where the latter may give more information that could influence the prognosis and treatment of the disease. Bitewings provide accurate geometry and details. Bitewings radiographs that have already been indicated for detecting caries can be used to assess bone levels around teeth without further radiation exposure. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Issues of Concern -- Exposure Dose
retrieved_docs.content: Voxels in dental cone beam computed tomography imaging are rectangular cuboids with sides varying in length from 0.08 to 0.4 mm. The field of view (FOV) width can range between 4 to 20 cm, and its height can range from 3 to 20 cm. The tube voltage can be set between 60 and 120 kV, and the current can range from 1 to 10 mA. The duration of each imaging session can vary between 5 to 40 seconds. [5] These parameters differ considerably depending on the device and its release date.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Issues of Concern -- Cone Beam Computed Tomography General Recommendations
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging with cone beam computed tomography before implant surgery is more useful than post-implant imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Issues of Concern -- Cone Beam Computed Tomography General Recommendations
retrieved_docs.content: 2D radiography or plain radiography is the imaging modality of choice. However, cone beam computed tomography should be considered when the diagnosis cannot be adequately made with 2D imaging. When utilizing cone beam computed tomography, it is vital to use established criteria to select an appropriate field of view (FOV).

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Implantology
retrieved_docs.content: For HUs on CBCT to be used, the accuracy of the HU should be known. With more advanced CBCT software and methods, it should be possible to improve the accuracy of CBCT HU values when determining bone densities at implant sites. Cone beam computed tomography provides a subjective assessment of bone quality, not an objective one. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Implantology
retrieved_docs.content: Bone density can be obtained from CT units and expressed in Hounsfield units (HU). This is not part of the system international (SI) system but is a practical unit representing the relative deviation of the measured linear attenuation of material from that of water. Unlike CT scans, CBCT does not allow for the measurement of bone density in Hounsfield units. Methods have been proposed to convert CT numbers measured on cone beam computed tomography scans to HU. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Implantology
retrieved_docs.content: Bone quality and reports on implant success and failure are used in implant treatment. The quality and quantity of bone available at the implant site are critical local patient factors in determining the success of dental implants. [14] Factors like bone density, skeletal size, bone architecture, the 3D orientation of the trabecula, and matrix properties conform bone quality. It is not only a matter of mineral content but also of structure.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Issues of Concern -- Patient Positioning
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the cone beam computed tomography machine, patients may stand, sit, or lie on a table. In-office 2D imaging usually involves sitting or standing. For 3D cone beam imaging, minimizing patient movement is instrumental in obtaining high-quality images and reducing picture blur and motion. Dentists should consider this when choosing the appropriate patient positioning for cone beam computed tomography imaging. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Issues of Concern -- Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Other imaging techniques, like medical-grade CT or MRI, should be used to diagnose soft tissue pathology instead of dental cone beam computed tomography. The smallest possible FOV should be chosen during imaging to minimize radiation exposure. A dental radiologist may be consulted to increase the diagnostic accuracy when imaging a large area and when a lesion suspected to be a tumor is found with small-field imaging. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: chest” connects the chest to specific imaging modalities—“CT angiography of thorax with contrast,” “Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast,” and the sub‑procedure “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material.” The “Subdivision of thorax” also maintains a relationship with chest CT, underscoring its role in guiding imaging of thoracic sub‑regions. The mediastinum is the direct procedural site for specialized CT examinations that evaluate mediastinal‑pleural communications: “thoracic CT scan: mediastinal‑pleural fistula,” as well as laterality‑specific studies (“mediastinal‑pleural fistula bilaterally,” “on right,” and “on left”). The modality “ct scan chest” is employed as the finding method for a range of CT observations, including “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” “mass of posterior mediastinum,” “mass of superior mediastinum,” “mediastinal mass,” “mediastinal mass containing calcium,” and “mediastinal effusion.” All

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 778
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast, computed tomography of the thorax performed diagnostically without contrast material, and computerized axial tomography of the thorax with contrast are all specific subtypes of a chest CT scan. Each of these imaging procedures serves as a method for identifying mediastinal abnormalities. In particular, computed tomography of the chest can reveal a mass of the mid mediastinum, a mass of the posterior mediastinum, a mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, or a mediastinal mass containing calcium. All of these findings are classified as mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية) and are documented as having been detected by a chest CT scan. The relationship among these entities underscores that various CT‑based techniques—whether with or without contrast—are employed to locate and characterize mediastinal masses, including their specific anatomic location and the presence of calcification, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and subsequent patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast, Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast, Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, and Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections are all specific forms of a ct scan chest. These chest CT techniques constitute the primary imaging method for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Using a ct scan chest, radiologists can identify several types of mediastinal lesions that are each classified as كتلة منصفية. The lesions described include computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mass of posterior mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mass of superior mediastinum, computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass, and computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass containing calcium. Each of these mediastinal masses is detected by the ct scan chest, establishing a direct “has_finding_method” relationship between the imaging study and the identification of كتلة منصفية in the mid, posterior, superior, or calcium‑containing mediastinum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specific types of chest CT scans. Diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material, as well as diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and additional sections, represent narrower sub‑categories within the broader chest CT imaging modality. In addition, computed tomography of the chest is used as a finding method for detecting miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure of chest CT techniques and illustrate how particular CT protocols are applied for detailed diagnostic evaluation of thoracic pathology.
